1: "The fool says in his heart, 'There is no God.' They are corrupt, their deeds are vile; there is no one who does good" — Paul cites this verse as part of his comprehensive indictment in Romans 3:10-12: "There is no one righteous, not even one... there is no one who does good, not even one." The diagnosis is universal — and universally applicable, pointing to the absolute need for a righteousness that comes not from human performance but from divine gift. Christ is "the righteousness of God" made available to all who believe (Romans 3:21-22; 1 Corinthians 1:30).
2: "The LORD looks down from heaven on all mankind to see if there are any who understand, any who seek God" — the divine survey that finds no one righteous. The gospel responds to this divine survey: God found what he was looking for — not in a fallen human being but in the eternal Son who became human. Christ "sought" the Father perfectly (John 8:29: "I always do what pleases him"), "understood" the divine will fully (John 5:19-20), and became the one human being who met the divine assessment. In him, those who seek God are gathered.
3: "All have turned away, all have become corrupt; there is no one who does good, not even one" — Paul quotes Psalms 14:1-3 in Romans 3:10-12 to establish the universal scope of sin, making Christ's atoning work necessary for all. The "not even one" is absolute — there is no natural exception, no culture or era where human goodness suffices. This universality of need creates the universality of the gospel's offer: "for all have sinned and fall short of the glory of God, and all are justified freely by his grace through the redemption that came by Christ Jesus" (Romans 3:23-24).
4: "Do all these evildoers know nothing? They devour my people as though eating bread" — the persecution of God's people. Christ identified himself with the persecuted: "Whatever you did for one of the least of these brothers and sisters of mine, you did for me" (Matthew 25:40). And more directly on the Damascus road: "Saul, Saul, why do you persecute me?" (Acts 9:4) — the risen Christ's identification with his persecuted body was total. To devour Christ's people is to devour Christ himself.
5: "But there they are, overwhelmed with dread, for God is present in the company of the righteous" — the sudden reversal: the persecutors are overwhelmed when they discover that God himself is present with those they are attacking. This is the great reversal of the cross and resurrection: those who crucified Jesus thought they were eliminating a threat; they discovered (Acts 2:36: "God has made this Jesus, whom you crucified, both Lord and Messiah") that they had attacked the one in whom God was fully present (Colossians 2:9: "in Christ all the fullness of the Deity lives in bodily form").
6: "You evildoers frustrate the plans of the poor, but the LORD is their refuge" — Christ as refuge for the poor, whose plans are consistently frustrated by the powerful. Luke 1:52-53 (the Magnificat): "He has brought down rulers from their thrones but has lifted up the humble. He has filled the hungry with good things but has sent the rich away empty." Christ reversed the social order of frustration: the poor whose plans are frustrated by the powerful are the ones whose prayers he hears and whose cause he champions.
7: "Oh, that salvation for Israel would come out of Zion! When the LORD restores his people, let Jacob rejoice and Israel be glad!" — the eschatological cry answered in Christ. Romans 11:26: "And in this way all Israel will be saved. As it is written: 'The deliverer will come from Zion; he will turn godlessness away from Jacob.'" Christ came from Zion (in the sense of the covenant people) and will return to restore fully. The prayer of Psalms 14:7 is the Advent prayer — oh, that the salvation would come — answered by "it is finished" (John 19:30) and awaiting its completion at the second coming.
